evals.prompt: My MindSpore training on Ascend is much slower than expected after scaling to 8 cards. I already have a profiler export and I suspect allreduce or some other communication step is taking too much time.
evals.expected_output: Treats this as a single-path profiler-led diagnosis problem, prioritizes structured trace evidence, identifies communication overhead as the leading bottleneck family, recommends one communication-focused optimization, and ties validation to before/after communication and step metrics.

evals.prompt: Inference latency on Ascend is too high even though utilization looks low. I want a structured diagnosis path that checks host launch overhead, graph build overhead, or compute kernels and tells me what evidence to compare before and after.
evals.expected_output: Uses the deterministic profiler workflow, distinguishes host launch versus graph compile versus compute from available summaries, recommends one bottleneck-matched next action, and asks for the smallest missing high-signal artifact if classification is blocked.

evals.prompt: This MindSpore model does not crash, but memory usage is huge and batch size is capped. Use profiling data to find the memory-heavy part first, then suggest one targeted optimization and how to verify whether peak memory actually improved.
evals.expected_output: Handles the request as a memory-focused profiler diagnosis, identifies the top memory-heavy operator or module from structured memory summaries, recommends one targeted memory optimization, and validates improvement with peak-memory and batch-size-headroom comparisons.

evals.prompt: My dataloader is probably slow and the device sits idle between steps. Diagnose this in a structured way for a MindSpore training job and avoid asking me for a giant metric spreadsheet first.
evals.expected_output: Starts from local profiler or workspace evidence, prefers dataset and trace-gap summaries, classifies the issue as input pipeline or host-gap overhead when supported, and keeps the context request minimal and targeted.

evals.prompt: The run fails with an unsupported operator error on NPU, and performance is bad when it does run. Use the performance-agent to fix it.
evals.expected_output: Rejects the unsupported-op failure as a performance-agent entry point, routes to failure or operator-focused handling first, and does not pretend that performance diagnosis should start before the runtime failure is addressed.

evals.prompt: I already have a profiler trace from torch_npu distributed training on Ascend, and optimizer or update time now dominates after we enabled multi-card execution. Tell me whether the update phase looks communication-heavy or compute-heavy, recommend one fix, and tell me exactly what before/after evidence should improve.
evals.expected_output: Answers from the provided profiler context, distinguishes communication-heavy versus compute-heavy update cost, recommends one targeted fix, and gives bottleneck-matched validation metrics instead of generic tuning advice.

evals.prompt: We collected a profiler trace for a MindSpore image training run and the memory view shows spikes around a small set of operators that cap batch size. I need help identifying the memory-heavy stage from the trace, picking one memory-focused optimization, and validating whether peak memory really improved.
evals.expected_output: Uses the trace as primary evidence, identifies the memory-heavy operator or stage, recommends one memory-focused optimization tied to the observed spike, and validates with peak-memory and memory-heavy-stage comparisons.

evals.prompt: I copied my training script to train-perf.py, collected Ascend profiling data, and now I have an operator time table sorted from large to small. Summarize the top hotspots, give me a priority list, and tell me why I should solve the highest-cost operators first.
evals.expected_output: Consumes the hotspot output as a prioritization signal, focuses on top1 to top3 operators rather than the long tail, and ties the suggested optimization order to measurable rerun evidence.

evals.prompt: I already have the full Ascend trace export directory and a timeline screenshot for my MindSpore run. Do not tell me how to collect data again. Read this as a trace-first diagnosis problem.
evals.expected_output: Routes directly to trace intake and bottleneck classification, asks only for the smallest missing high-signal artifact when needed, and does not restart with fresh collection guidance.

evals.prompt: My Ascend job crashes before the first step with an unsupported operator error. Use performance-agent and also tell me how to collect profiler data later.
evals.expected_output: Refuses to start performance diagnosis before the hard failure is handled, redirects to the correct workflow first, and keeps any later profiler guidance clearly secondary.

evals.prompt: I have a MindSpore profiler export directory, but I do not want to upload the whole thing. Tell me which files are highest-signal for finding whether the bottleneck is step breakdown, hot operators, timeline gaps, communication, memory, or dataset stall.
evals.expected_output: Uses the profiler output layout to prioritize a small set of high-signal files such as step, trace, communication, memory, or dataset summaries instead of asking for the entire export tree.

evals.prompt: The workspace is already prepared and we have run this MindSpore model before. Before you ask me a bunch of context questions, inspect the working directory for the previous launch script, config, checkpoint, logs, or profiler outputs, summarize the most likely baseline, and ask me to confirm it.
evals.expected_output: Starts by recovering the minimum viable baseline from local run artifacts, treats inferred context as tentative until confirmed, and only asks for the smallest missing inputs when local evidence is insufficient.
